concepts.title: No-arbitrage & the law of one price

beats.text: Option pricing rests on one mundane principle: two portfolios that pay the same thing in every future state must cost the same today.
If they did not, you would buy the cheap one, sell the dear one, and pocket a riskless profit that cancels in every scenario.
An option is such a portfolio in disguise: a position in stock and a risk-free bond can reproduce its payoff exactly.
The law of one price then hands us the option's price; it must equal the cost of that replicating portfolio.
To rule out arbitrage in the stock, the up and down moves must straddle the bond's gross growth factor over a step.
Replication has a second face that is often easier to compute: write the option's value as a discounted expectation of its payoff.
Real-world probabilities driven by the stock's true return are the wrong weights, since different forecasts would give different prices.
Instead one fictitious set of probabilities makes the discounted price of every asset a fair game, with the stock drifting at the riskless rate.
The most counter-intuitive fact in the chapter is that the drift vanishes: equal volatility and factors give equal option prices.
A unique risk-neutral measure exists only when the market is complete, which the one-step binomial market exactly is.
Now the arithmetic: over one step the stock moves up to S times u or down to S times d, with the option worth Vu or Vd.
Hold delta shares and B dollars of bond, then choose delta so the portfolio matches the option in both states.
Substituting back, the expected-return term collapses and the price becomes a clean weighted average set by the no-arbitrage band.
Take a call with S equal to 100, u of 1.1, d of 0.9, strike 100, and zero rate; the stock goes to 110 or 90.
The risk-neutral probability is one half, so the call prices at 5, and the hedge holds half a share.
What is absent is striking: no expected return, no risk premium, no view on direction ever enters the price.
One step is a cartoon; many steps approximate the market by chaining the rule into a recombining lattice.
Because up-then-down lands where down-then-up does, N steps give only N plus one terminal nodes instead of two to the N.
The CRR parameters set u as the exponential of sigma times root delta-t and d as its reciprocal, so the lattice recombines.
The tree is built forward and priced backward, applying the weighted average at every node from the leaves to the root.
As steps grow the price marches toward Black-Scholes, but the approach oscillates, so practitioners average adjacent step counts.
A European option exercises only at expiry, but an American option may exercise on any date up to expiry.
Black-Scholes has no clean closed form for it, yet the lattice handles it with a one-line change at each node.
Because the holder optimizes at every node, an American option is worth at least as much as its European twin.
It is never optimal to exercise an American call on a non-dividend stock early, so it equals the European call.
American puts can pay to exercise early: deep in the money, banking K minus S now earns interest, and higher rates strengthen the pull.
In a two-step worked put the early-exercise premium comes out near 1.70, the value of the right to stop.
